There’s a design flaw. Once an app has been approved by the parents, it’s treated as permanently approved. There’s no way to change that, only to block its usage. And here comes a second design flaw: the child can simply uninstall the app and reinstall it. They no longer need parental approval, and on top of that it bypasses all the restrictions such as screen-time limits, blocked apps, and so on. This is wonderful and has solved many of the tablet problems in our house. I have a few suggested feature additions to make it even better, Google:  1). Allow multiple shut-off & turn-on times during the day rather than just a simple bed-time schedule. That way we could allow morning use until it's time to leave for school and have it locked down until school's out.   2). Allow time limits for certain apps. Or exempt some apps from the time limit. For example, I might allow unlimited use for an educational app, but I might limit all other apps to 2 hours per day.  3). Allow a parent to send a message to the tablet, similar to a text message. "DINNER IN 10 MINUTES! SAVE YOUR GAME BECAUSE I'M ABOUT TO PUSH ***THE BUTTON***!"  4). Give the parent the option to add a pre-defined amount of time once limits have been reached rather than just hit "unlock." That way I can give my kids an extra 10 minutes without having to revisit it 10 minutes later.    Otherwise, parents - this is a great app and I highly recommend it! The fact that it's free is even better.

I love the idea of this app & was excited to use it- but it has a major flaw. There is no option for opting out of Y kids. This is such a problem for us we had to remove the app. While YT kids is great for really young kids, for my pre teen boys it is to restrictive. So many of their favorite gamers are restricted & even favorite science channels. While I understand promoting YTkids, I don't understand why there isn't an option to opt out? If there was I would definitely use this app.

Family Link has been a total disaster for the last year I've tried to use it. I wanted to manage my kids' Chromebooks that they're also using for school, for which they also have a separate Google account. Of course Family Link can't manage those accounts, and I understand the reasons why, that's OK. The main problem was that the Chromebooks would keep requiring the parent code randomly, when the kids were trying to login to their non-managed accounts, and at times when even the managed accounts shouldn't have required a parent code anyway. This was just a huge annoyance while they were at home, and effectively locks them out completely when they take the device to school. So I disabled management, but that doesn't always solve the problem either. Several times I experienced that it was still asking for the damn parent code. I think I have gotten rid of it on their main devices, but now this happened again today on an older device we haven't used in a while. So I went to dig out the parent code, and the app is requiring me to set everything up again. The UI won't proceed until I have a device and a child account to set up... I just want the damn parent code! This is so broken that I'm just going to hard reset the device and start over. My advice: don't let Family Link near your Chromebook. I'm a software engineer and Google enthusiast and this has been nothing but frustration for me.

Look, the app is fine, I can see what the kids are doing, monitor apps and usage etc. BUT it is an absolute joke that Google Play gift cards can't be uploaded to a child's account, OR be used as a payment method for apps or in-app purchases. I've spent hours trying to find a work around but apparently this has been a major issue for years and nothing has been done to change or fix this huge problem. I don't know why Google bother having gift cards, except to rip people off?

I loved this app until the update. It worked exactly as needed. It was quick to change a time or give bonus time. No issues at all. review_text: I was really hoping that this would work but it doesn't. Not in the way you think it will. ----------------------------------------- I installed this app on my daughter's phone to not only manage what kind of apps she can download, but to also manage the amount of time she spends on it. I wanted to give her the responsibility of managing her time but make sure there is some kind of oversight. However, even though I have set usage limits and bedtime hours, it would seem that even using the YouTube app will override those time limits with ease. How do I know this? Because if I take her phone from her with the app that is still active and press the home button, the phone will lock immediately with a screen notifying that the bedtime hours are in effect. And it's not just YouTube either. She has learnt, and so have I, that by just keeping any app active, no matter how long she's been on her phone for or what the time may be, the usage and bedtime hours will not lock her phone down at all. This completely negates the point of a huge part of this app. Currently the best you can hope for is seeing what your child has been using, how long for, where they are if they're not home and being able to unlock their handset without knowing their pin should you need to. As for usage limits and bedtime hours? Its useless because, again, apps on the device will override those limits. ----------------------------------------- Overall this app could have, and should have, been amazing. I haven't used an iPhone for years so cannot comment on any iOS firmware beyond iOS 10. However, what I have experienced of the parental controls for iOS devices up to iOS 10 is that they are shockingly lacking. This is one of the reasons why I chose an Android device for her because of what this app was supposed to offer me as a parent. The reality is that this app does not work like you expect it to so that is why I have given it such a low score. ---------------------------------- Just to update this review, my daughter showed me how she was able to access the device passed the usage limits and bedtime hours. All she had to do was press the 'Emergency' button, then press the button to acces multiple application windows and then quickly press the app that was active. The app comes up and allows her to carry on where she left off regardless what limits I had set. Restarting the device also allows normal access beyond the time limits and bedtime hours. Sometimes it's brief and other times the phone can be accessed and operated normally for at least a couple of limits. ---------------------------------- The devices that the Family Link app is installed on are a Samsung Galaxy S8 (parent) Samsung Galaxy S6 (child).

review_text: This has been nothing but a headache. I really wanted to like it, but nothing worked quite right. The kids have Samsung devices and the app prevents anything Samsung from updating. Come on, just let me install the regular YouTube app for my kid(s) if I want to. This should be my choice. Instead they bug me to use my PC or Laptop. The whole reason I got them a ChromeBook was to get them off my devices. The Family Link features are great for locking the device or tracking time, but not being able to Install certain apps like YouTube is ruining the entire Chromebook experience. That and not being able to install Minecraft...but that is a separate issue.

In theory a good thing. In praxis this is the usual Google solution that just doesn't work that well. Setting up a child was really hard and my wife to this day cannot accept the invite. We cannot share bought movies or games across the family and cannot set up a family payment option. I do like though that I can set time limits and block certain apps.

Seriously Google, it's time to implement app categories. Parents need to lock certain apps on a child's phone (entertainment, games, social media, etc.), while leaving other applications unlocked (phone, messaging, maps, etc.). In fact, this feature is long overdue - all other parental control apps worth their weight implemented this option years ago. iOS has it natively built in. This is a deal breaker for me, as I look to update my child's phone. I may have to make the switch to iPhone, though I prefer Android. But...I'm a parent, and I have to make the best decision for my kids. Family Link just isn't cutting it without the app category feature.
